title: Technical: Anatomy of a Template
subtitle: A back-to-front walkthrough of the open-source application template that ships with the Well-Built Harness, and why the best specification is working code.
abstract: The Well-Built Harness ships a working application, not an empty folder: a full-stack monorepo, a Vue 3 client and an Express server over PostgreSQL, that builds, signs users in, talks to a database, streams live updates, and passes its tests and security scanners the day it is copied. This appendix is a walkthrough of how it works, back to front, for an engineer or DBA. The premise is simple: the best specification is code. A document describes intent; the template proves it, and an AI extending a secure, standards-built baseline has far less room to introduce a flaw. It covers the server middleware chain, sign-in and tokens, CSRF and cookies, the route-to-database path, real-time events, the single API client, routing guards, composables, the interface, accessibility, and the build.

slides.title: Anatomy of a Template

slides.subcaption: The application that ships with the Well-Built Harness.
slides.text: The Well-Built Harness ships a working application, not an empty folder. The template is a full-stack Vue 3, Express, and Postgres project that builds, signs users in, talks to a database, streams live updates, and passes its tests and security scanners the day it is copied. This appendix walks through how it works, back to front.

slides.title: The best specification is code
slides.caption: The argument
slides.subcaption: Working code proves what a system does; a document only describes it.
slides.text: Ask an AI to add authentication and it must invent a token format, a lifetime, a storage choice, a refresh strategy, and a role model. Each choice is a chance to be wrong. The template has already made those choices, in code, to a known standard, so the AI works inside a decided system instead of inventing one, and the application is compliant before the AI writes a line.

slides.title: Decided once, centralised
slides.caption: What the AI inherits

items.label: Identity
items.desc: SSO sign-in, RS256 tokens in httpOnly cookies, refresh rotation, role-based access.

items.label: Safety
items.desc: Parameterized SQL, double-submit CSRF, security headers, one safe error path.

items.label: One API client
items.desc: Every request retries refresh, CSRF, and rate limits in one place, so no screen can get it wrong.

items.label: Real-time
items.desc: Server-Sent Events for live updates, with the list endpoint as the source of truth.

slides.text: The dangerous decisions are made once, in code, and centralised so they cannot be re-made wrongly. Identity, CSRF, parameterized SQL, the response shape, error handling, and the API client's retry logic are single, tested implementations the AI inherits rather than authors.

slides.title: Built to the standards, up front
slides.caption: Compliant before the build

visual_config.stat_label: tests passing and failing the day the template is copied, plus six automated security scanners

slides.subcaption: Built to the OWASP ASVS Level 2 and WCAG 2.1 AA standards the harness enforces.
slides.text: Each of the OWASP Top 10 risks is addressed in the template's code, so a new application inherits the defence. The harness then checks, mechanically, that the work still meets the standard on every change.

sections.title: From the route to the database
The OWASP Top 10, addressed up front
What the template solves for the agent

blocks.text: The Well-Built Harness ships a working application, not an empty folder. The template is a full-stack monorepo, a Vue 3 client and an Express server over PostgreSQL, that builds, signs users in, talks to a database, streams live updates, and passes its tests and six security scanners on the day it is copied. This appendix is a walkthrough of how it works, back to front, for an engineer or DBA who reads code more readily than prose. The premise is simple: the best specification is code. A document describes intent; the template proves it, and an AI extending a secure, standards-built baseline has far less room to introduce a flaw. The application is compliant before the AI writes a line. This is the template at the centre of the [Well-Built Harness](/paper/p7p2k).

Ask an AI to add authentication and it has to choose a token format, a lifetime, a storage location, a refresh strategy, and a role model. Each choice is a chance to be wrong, and the security choices are the costly ones to get wrong. The template has already made them, in code, to a known standard, so the AI works inside a decided system rather than inventing one. The rest of this paper is what has been decided, and how the pieces fit together.

blocks.label: Starts green, not from zero
blocks.body: The template ships with 773 passing tests and zero failures, plus six automated security scanners. An application built from it inherits a working, tested, standards-compliant baseline on its first day.

blocks.text: The best specification is code. Give the AI a secure, standards-driven, accessible platform from the beginning, and it is far less likely to make a mistake. The solution is compliant before the AI even begins to build.
blocks.cite: Janak Alford, Deputy Minister, Ministry of Technology and Innovation

blocks.text: The server is a single Express application in TypeScript. Every request crosses the same ordered chain of middleware before it reaches a route, and the order is deliberate: cheap, broad protections first, identity and intent last.

blocks.title: The middleware chain, in order
rows: trust proxy
Honours the load balancer's client IP and protocol, so rate limits and secure cookies behave correctly behind a proxy.
Security headers (helmet)
Content-Security-Policy without unsafe-eval, HSTS for one year, and cross-origin isolation.
Permissions-Policy
Switches off camera, microphone, geolocation, payment, USB, and motion sensors.
Compression
gzip or brotli on responses; skipped for event streams so live updates are not buffered.
Static files
In production, serves the built client with a one-year immutable cache, before CORS.
CORS allow-list
Permits only configured origins, with credentials, and a fixed set of methods and headers.
Rate limiting
Caps the API at 200 requests per 15 minutes, with stricter limits on sign-in and AI routes.
Content-Type check
Rejects unexpected media types on POST, PUT, and PATCH with a 415.
Body parsing
Accepts JSON and form bodies, capped at one megabyte.
Cookies, correlation id, logging
Parses cookies, stamps a correlation id, logs every request, and warns when one takes over a second.
Passport
Initialised for OAuth sign-in only; no server-side sessions are used.
CSRF (global)
Validates state-changing requests by default, with a short, documented exempt list.
Routers, then the error handler
Versioned routes under /api/v1 (with an /api alias); a final handler turns any error into a safe response.

blocks.text: Inside a router, each route states its own guards in plain sequence. A public read declares none. A write declares all three: validate against a schema, require a valid session, and require a role. For example: router.post('/', validate(schema), authenticate, authorize('editor'), handler). The security of a route can be read off its signature, by a reviewer or by the AI.

Sign-in is delegated to Google and Microsoft through Passport and OpenID Connect; the template stores no passwords. On success the server issues two JSON Web Tokens signed with RS256, an asymmetric key pair, so the server can verify a token without holding anything that could forge one, and sets them as httpOnly cookies that scripts in the browser cannot read. The authenticate middleware reads the access token from the cookie and, when it has expired, returns 401 with the code TOKEN_EXPIRED, which is the client's signal to refresh. Refresh tokens are stored only as SHA-256 hashes in a refresh_token table; every use rotates the token and any token can be revoked, so a stolen refresh token has a short and closable life.

blocks.title: Tokens and cookies
rows: Access token
RS256 JWT, 15-minute life, httpOnly and sameSite=lax cookie. Carries user id, email, and role.
Refresh token
RS256 JWT, 7-day life, httpOnly cookie. Stored server-side only as a hash, rotated on every use, revocable.
Roles
viewer, submitter, editor, manager, admin, super_admin. authorize() compares position in that order; a database CHECK constraint mirrors the same list.
sameSite=lax
Lax, not strict, so the cross-site OAuth redirect still carries the cookie on the way back, while cross-site POSTs do not.
blocks.source: AIDE Common Harness template, utils/token.ts and utils/cookie-config.ts.

blocks.text: State-changing requests are protected by a double-submit check. The server sets an httpOnly csrf_token cookie and also returns the token value once, in the body of GET /auth/csrf-token. The client holds that value in memory only, never in storage, and echoes it in an X-CSRF-Token header. The middleware compares header to cookie with a constant-time comparison to avoid timing leaks. It is mounted globally, so a newly added route is protected by default rather than only when a developer remembers to opt in. The only exemptions are the OAuth start, the OAuth callback, and token refresh, each carrying a written reason it is safe: the callback is protected by the OAuth state and nonce, and refresh by the sameSite cookie plus rotation.

blocks.title: From the route to the database

blocks.text: Application logic is layered: route, then controller, then service, then model. The controller handles the request and shapes the reply, the service holds the business rules, and the model owns the SQL. Every query is parameterized with $1, $2, and so on; values are never concatenated into the statement, which closes SQL injection at the source. The model is also the only layer that maps API field names to database column names, so the wire format and the schema can change independently. Postgres is reached through a plain connection pool, with no object-relational layer hiding what actually runs.

Every response uses one envelope: success with a data payload, or failure with an error object carrying a code and a message. A single error handler catches everything thrown, maps known Postgres errors to safe HTTP codes (a unique-key violation becomes 409, a foreign-key violation 400), and in production strips stack traces and SQL from the reply. Async handlers are wrapped so a rejected promise reaches that error handler instead of crashing the process. The result is that error shape, status codes, and information hiding are uniform across every endpoint.

Live updates, an admin broadcast or a new notification, use Server-Sent Events, a one-way HTTP stream that is lighter than a WebSocket and reconnects on its own. On the server, a small manager keeps a map of user id to the set of that user's open streams, one per browser tab and capped at ten, and writes named notification events to them. On the client, useNotificationStream opens an EventSource with credentials so the auth cookie rides along, and listens for the named event; the default message handler does not fire for named events, which is a common trap. The stream is treated as best-effort: the source of truth stays the notifications list endpoint, so a dropped event is never a lost record. The stream closes on pagehide and reopens on pageshow, so an open connection does not disqualify the page from the browser's back-forward cache.

Every call from the browser goes through one configured axios instance. It sends credentials so cookies travel, attaches a request id and, on writes, the CSRF token, and centralises the three failures that every screen would otherwise have to handle alone. Because the logic lives in one file, no view reimplements it and no view can get it wrong. This is the clearest example of what the template settles on the AI's behalf.

blocks.title: What the one API client handles for every request
rows: 401, session expired
Calls /auth/refresh once, queues any concurrent requests behind it, then replays them. If refresh fails, it emits an auth:expired event and reports it for audit.
403, CSRF missing or mismatch
Re-fetches the CSRF token and retries the request once, queuing concurrent writes behind the refresh.
429, rate limited
Waits the interval named in the Retry-After header, capped at ten seconds, and retries once.
Any error, in production
Returns a generic message chosen by status code, so server internals never reach the browser.

blocks.text: The client router uses history mode and lazy-loads every view as its own bundle, so a page's code downloads only when it is visited. Each route carries metadata: whether it requires a signed-in user, whether it requires an admin, whether it is for guests only, which layout to use (default, admin, or blank), and a title. A single beforeEach guard loads the current user once if needed, sends unauthenticated users to sign-in while preserving where they were headed, keeps non-admins out of admin routes, and turns signed-in users away from the sign-in page. A sanitizeRedirect helper rejects any redirect target that is not a plain relative path, closing open-redirect attacks, and afterEach sets the document title.

There is one Pinia store, for authentication. It holds the user and the role helpers, signs out after thirty minutes idle, and listens for the auth:expired event the API client emits. Everything else is a composable, which is the template's data-access pattern. useFetch(url) wraps a GET and returns data, loading, error, and a refresh function; the feature composables, useServices, useResources, useForms, useFiles, and the rest, wrap their endpoints the same way, so a view binds to a uniform shape instead of calling the API directly. useTheme persists light or dark mode in local storage, usePwa drives the install and update prompts, useToast wraps notifications, and useNotificationStream feeds the live counter. User-supplied HTML is run through DOMPurify before it is ever rendered. Forms are built with FormKit behind one shared, themed configuration, so validation and styling are consistent rather than hand-rolled per screen.

The interface is PrimeVue 4 on the Aura theme, with components auto-imported by a resolver so only the ones used are bundled, over Tailwind 4 utility styling. Application start-up wires Pinia, PrimeVue, FormKit, and the router, registers a global error handler that surfaces a toast, preloads the icon font to shorten the first render, and mounts the app only after the user session and CSRF token are fetched. Dark mode is a class on the root element.

Accessibility is built in rather than added later: a skip-to-content link, a live region that announces each route change to screen readers, and conformance to the WCAG 2.1 AA standard the harness enforces. The build splits vendor code into separate chunks, Vue, PrimeVue, charts, and maps, so each is cached on its own; it minifies and drops console and debugger statements in production; and it serves built assets with a one-year immutable cache. The application is an installable progressive web app: a service worker precaches the shell, falls back to the app for client routes but never for the API, and prompts the user to update when a new build ships. The layout is responsive on mobile by default, and the manifest includes a maskable icon for a clean home-screen install.

blocks.title: The OWASP Top 10, addressed up front

blocks.text: The OWASP Top 10 is the security industry's list of the ten most serious classes of web vulnerability. The template addresses each one in code, built to the OWASP Application Security Verification Standard at Level 2, the standard the harness holds the work to.

blocks.title: How the template addresses the OWASP Top 10 (2021)
rows: A01 Broken access control
Every protected route runs authenticate then authorize against the role hierarchy; inactive accounts are refused at refresh.
A02 Cryptographic failures
RS256-signed tokens, SHA-256-hashed refresh tokens, httpOnly and secure cookies, forced HTTPS, and optional column encryption.
A03 Injection
All database access is parameterized, and every request is validated against a schema before use.
A04 Insecure design
CSRF on by default, refresh-token rotation, tiered rate limits, and a server that refuses to start with unsafe settings.
A05 Security misconfiguration
Strict security headers, configuration validated at startup, and errors stripped of internal detail in production.
A06 Vulnerable components
Dependencies pinned, with known-bad transitive versions overridden.
A07 Authentication failures
Short-lived access tokens, rotating refresh tokens, rate-limited sign-in, and single-tenant enforcement.
A08 Data integrity failures
Token signatures and OAuth state are verified, and uploads are checked by declared type and actual content.
A09 Logging and monitoring failures
Structured logs with correlation ids and an audit trail of sensitive actions.
A10 Server-side request forgery
Not specifically hardened in the template; flagged so the application closes it deliberately.
blocks.source: AIDE Common Harness template, mapped to OWASP Top 10 (2021).

blocks.title: What the template solves for the agent

blocks.text: Read back to front, the pattern is consistent. The dangerous decisions are made once, in code, and centralised so they cannot be re-made wrongly: identity, CSRF, parameterized SQL, the response envelope, error sanitization, the live-update fallback, and the API client's retry logic are each a single, tested implementation the AI inherits rather than writes. That is what it means to treat code as the specification. The AI is handed a compliant system and asked to extend it, and the harness checks mechanically that it still holds on every change, the same discipline the [Anti-Drift Harness](/paper/of1cj) applies as the codebase grows.

The template is honest about its edges. Malware scanning of uploaded files is a switch to wire to a real scanner. Rate-limit state is held in memory until a shared store is configured for more than one instance. These are documented, not hidden, so the application closes them on purpose rather than discovering them by accident. A good foundation does not pretend to be finished; it states plainly where it ends and the building begins. The stack is open source throughout, Vue, Express, Postgres and their well-supported libraries, so there is no vendor lock-in and a large community keeps it patched, which is the baseline a public-facing government application must meet before it ships.
